title: Chapter 11: The Hollow Men
content: The fog was thinner than it had been in weeks.

I noticed it the way you notice a song has stopped playing — not by its presence, but by the strange shape its absence leaves. The fog was still there, of course. It clung to the low ground along Millhaven Road and pooled in the gutters like something spilled and not yet cleaned. But the wall of it, the fortress of white-grey that had hemmed Blackwood Vale since September, had retreated. Just barely. Just enough for me to see the hedge line on the opposite side of the street, the cracked mailbox at the end of the Culverths' drive, the dead oak with its one living branch.
I should have felt relieved. Instead, I felt watched.

I was walking home from the shop. Thursdays I closed at six — Marguerite had insisted, back when she was still insisting things, that one early night a week kept the blood from thickening. I'd kept the habit even after she stopped insisting anything at all. The bag of receipt books and restocking lists hung from my wrist, and the cold was the kind that didn't bite so much as settle — a flat, patient cold that sat on your skin and waited for you to stop generating enough heat to push it off.
That was when I saw them.

They stood at the edge of the fog where it met the tree line behind the Henderson lot. Seven of them. Arranged in no formation I could name — not a line, not a semicircle, nothing so deliberate. They simply were, the way stones are, or fence posts. Dark suits. Not black, but the colour of deep water at night. Ties drawn neat. Shoes polished to a gloss that caught no light because there was no light to catch.
And no faces.

I stopped walking. The receipt bag swung against my thigh and I didn't feel it. My eyes were doing what eyes do when they encounter something they can't resolve — darting, refocusing, trying to find the trick. A mask. A trick of the fog. A migraine aura, a smearing of the visual field. But there was no trick. Where faces should have been — brows, eyes, noses, mouths, jaws — there was only smooth grey skin. Unbroken. Like clay pressed flat with a thumb. No orifices. No expression. No person.
Just watching.

I knew they were watching. I couldn't see eyes. There was nothing to see with. But the weight of their attention pressed against me like a hand on my chest, and I understood — with a certainty that had no source in logic or experience — that they had been waiting for me. Not for anyone. For me, specifically, tonight, on this road, at this hour. As if my walking home from the shop at dusk on a Thursday was a line in a ledger somewhere, and I had just checked the box.
The curse shifted.

I didn't feel it change so much as I felt the direction of it reverse. For weeks the curse had been a pull — attraction, want, the gravitational drag toward Julian's studio and Julian's hands and Julian's voice saying my name like it was a word he'd invented. I'd fought it. I'd built walls in my mind. I'd repeated Marguerite's warnings like catechism. And I'd held. Barely, but I'd held.
Now the pull became a push.

It wasn't gentler. It was worse. An invisible hand between my shoulder blades, steady and enormous, pressing me toward Julian's studio with a mechanical insistence that felt nothing like desire. Desire had a shape — it curved and swelled and whispered and promised. This was flat. Procedural. The way you push a cart down an aisle because the list says you need milk. No seduction in it. No warmth. Just pressure, and direction.
*Come to me.*

The voice came from the fog, and it came from inside my skull, and it came from the place behind my own thoughts where the curse lived — all three at once, all three the same. But I knew — the way I'd known they were watching — that it wasn't Julian speaking. Julian's voice was warm even at its worst. Even when he'd said things designed to wound, there'd been blood in it, the heat of a living throat. This was different. Older. Something that had been speaking long before Julian existed and would continue speaking long after he was gone. Julian was a mouth. Someone — something — else was the voice behind it.

The curse was engineered. The word arrived in my mind with the blunt clarity of a diagnosis. Not fate. Not the tragic machinery of generational loss. Not even love, or the poison parody of it that the Buena women had been drowning in for two hundred and sixty years. A mechanism. A design. Someone had built this.
I ran.

Not toward home. Not toward the cottage and its wards and its herbs and Marguerite's voice on the answering machine saying *Aurelia, whatever you do —* No. I ran toward the fog. Toward the figures. Because if someone had built this, then someone could answer for it, and I was sick — genuinely, flamingly sick — of being the only one in the room who didn't know the terms.

The fog didn't part for me. It swallowed me. The cold of it was different inside — wetter, denser, tasting of iron and old stone. I could hear my own footsteps stop making sound, as if the air itself had gone deaf. And then I was among them, or as close as I could get before my legs locked and I stood, panting, five feet from the nearest figure.

Up close, the grey was worse. The skin — if it was skin — had a texture like vellum, like something once alive that had been stretched and cured and scraped clean. There were no pores. No hairline. No ears. Just that unbroken plane of grey from the collar to the crown, smooth as a manufactured thing. The suit was real — I could see the stitching at the lapel, the subtle sheen of wool. Someone had dressed them. Or they had dressed themselves. The detail was obscene. A faceless thing in a well-tailored suit, as if the absence of a face were a choice, not a deformity.
One of them raised a hand.

Not threatening. Not fast. The gesture was almost gentle — the way a conductor raises a baton before the first note, or a priest raises a palm to bless. Beckoning. Patient. The patience of something that has all the time in the world because it is not subject to time.
*The contract stands.*

The voice didn't come from the figure. It came from the fog, from the ground, from the iron taste in my mouth, from the marrow of my bones. It was neither male nor female. It was the voice of a document being read aloud. The voice of a seal being pressed into wax.
*Twenty-two. The seventh. The terms are clear.*

Twenty-two. I was the twenty-second Buena woman. The seventh — something. Seventh generation? Seventh Aurelia? The words were a citation, a clause reference, a line item in an agreement I had never signed and could not read. But they knew it. They knew every comma. They had been standing in this fog since before I was born, waiting for me to walk home on a Thursday so they could remind me that the paperwork was in order.
Procedural. Impersonal. Bureaucratic.

I opened my mouth to say something — a question, a curse, a name — and the fog surged. The figures didn't move. They didn't need to. The fog moved for them, and the fog pushed me back, gentle and inexorable as a turnstile, and I was on Millhaven Road again, and they were gone, and the fog had closed behind me like a door.

I didn't remember the walk to the cottage. I remembered my hand on the gate, the creak of it, the way the porch light flickered twice — its dying ritual — and then I was inside with the door shut and my back against it and my chest heaving.
The contract stands. Twenty-two. The seventh. The terms are clear.

There had to be a record. There had to be something in Marguerite's things, in the cottage, in the history the Buena women had been writing in journal after journal for two hundred years, that explained the terms. I went to the attic first. I pulled down the boxes I'd already searched three times. Rosa. Eloise. Catherine. Marguerite. Four journals, four women, four variations on the same annihilating story. I'd read them all. I'd memorised the passages that mattered. And none of them — not one — mentioned contracts. Terms. Clauses. Any of the language the figures had spoken in the fog.
Because I was looking in the wrong place.

The thought came to me at three in the morning, after two hours of sitting on the attic floor with Rosa's journal open on my lap and the words blurring into the grain of the page. I wasn't supposed to find another journal in the attic. The attic was where the Buena women kept their records — that was the tradition, that was the system. But the Hollow Council had a system too, and their system depended on the Buena women recording only what they were meant to record, passing down only what reinforced the mechanism.
What if one of them had hidden something outside the system?

I went downstairs to my own bedroom. I'd been sleeping in this room since I was seventeen, since Marguerite brought me here after my mother — after everything. I knew every crack in the ceiling, every water stain shaped like a continent on the wall above the headboard. I knew the sound the radiator made at two a.m. I knew the specific weight of the silence at night, how it pressed against the windows like something wanting in.
I did not know my floorboards.

I found it by accident, or by instinct, or by the curse working in ways I still couldn't predict. I was pacing — the adrenaline from the fog hadn't drained, was still cycling through me like something electric and ungrounded — and my heel caught the edge of the board beside the bed. The board lifted. Not rotted, not loose from age. Fitted. Cut with precision and laid back into place so that the seam was invisible unless you knew to press at the corner nearest the nightstand.

Under the board: a journal. Smaller than the others. Bound in navy fabric, not the cracked leather of the attic journals. Lighter. As if it had been protected from the damp and the years by something more than luck.

I lifted it out. The cold of the floorboard lingered on my fingertips — raw, mineral, the temperature of things that haven't seen sunlight in decades. The journal smelled of face powder and train tickets. That specific combination: the sweet chalky rot of vintage powder compact residue and the chemical tang of old paper pulped with lignin, the smell that rises from boxes of saved tickets in attics — small rectangles of journey, departure and arrival, all the places someone went trying to get away.
Vivienne Buena. 1932.
I opened it, and I read.
*He left and I WAITED. That was my gift — patience. I could make time slow or speed for myself. And I used it to WAIT for a man who was never coming back the same.*

Patience. Not the virtue. The ability. Vivienne's gift hadn't been endurance or fortitude or any of the words the eulogists like to dress grief in. It had been time itself — the power to stretch or compress her own experience of it. She could make an hour feel like a day. She could make a year pass like a weekend. She could live a lifetime in an afternoon or drag a single morning into the texture of a full season.
And she had used it to wait.

*I stretched three years into a decade in my own mind. I lived ten years of waiting while three passed for everyone else. Do you understand? I CHOSE this. I thought I was being faithful. I thought I was proving the depth of my love by making it last longer — by refusing to let time dilute what I felt. But time wasn't diluting it. Time was trying to SAVE me, and I wouldn't let it.*

*I would sit in this very room and slow the hours down until each one was a tunnel I had to crawl through. Every minute without him was a mile. Every silent evening was a continent. And I did it deliberately. I thought: if I can FEEL more waiting than any woman has ever felt, then my love will be the greatest love. Then he will have to come back. Then the universe will see and relent.*

*The universe did not see. The universe did not relent. The universe has a contract, and the contract does not care about the size of my love. It cares about the duration of my waiting. And I gave it more duration than any Buena woman before me.*

*I fed the curse. I FED it. With every hour I stretched, every day I inflated, I was giving the mechanism more of what it needed — more waiting, more submission, more time on my knees. The curse doesn't want my love. It wants my TIME. It wants me to STAY. And I made myself stay longer than anyone.*

My hands were shaking. I could feel the curse inside me — not the push now, not the pull, but something deeper. A lock turning. A door I hadn't known was there, swinging open on hinges that hadn't been oiled in ninety years.

*The gift is not patience. The gift is impatience. It was ALWAYS impatience. The ability to refuse to wait — to make time PASS for the things that need to end. I misunderstood. I did it backwards. I made myself more patient when I should have made myself less. I stretched the waiting when I should have compressed it into nothing — let the years flicker past like phone poles from a train window, let the days of absence blur and thin until they were too brief to hold pain.*

*I waited so long I forgot I was waiting. The waiting became the shape of my life. I couldn't see outside it anymore. Don't let that happen to you. Whoever is reading this — and I know someone will be, because there will always be another one of us, there will always be another Aurelia, the contract ensures it — DON'T WAIT. ACT. Let time do what it does — move forward. The curse is patient. You mustn't be.*
*The curse is patient.*

The lock turned all the way open, and I felt it — the gift, the curse, the mutation of one into the other, the thing Vivienne had done wrong not because she was weak but because she was doing what every woman in her position had been taught to do: endure, wait, be patient, hold on, give it time, he'll come around, love is worth the wait. She had taken the most dangerous advice the world gives women and weaponised it against herself with supernatural efficiency.
The impatience-curse unlocked.

I felt it like a clock starting — not the grinding of gears, but the sudden aware-existence of time as a substance, a medium, something I could touch and shape and accelerate. Not time in the world. Time in me. My experience of duration. The length of a moment. The weight of a day. I could compress the waiting into nothing. I could make the months of the curse pass in the space of a breath. I could refuse to wait.

But the gift didn't feel like freedom. It felt like a weapon given to a soldier who has just realised she's been conscripted. The Hollow Council's words echoed in the iron taste still lingering on my tongue: *The contract stands. Twenty-two. The seventh. The terms are clear.*

The terms were clear to them. Not to me. Not yet. But the shape of the truth was forming, the way a body forms in fog — you can't see the whole thing, but you can see enough to know it's not a trick of the light. It's real, and it's coming toward you, and it has no face.

Julian wasn't the enemy. Julian was a tool — a mechanism, the way I was a mechanism, the way we were all mechanisms in a machine that had been running since 1766. The Buena women weren't tragic heroines in a love story. We weren't even victims of a curse in the way I'd understood curses to work — an affliction, a hex, something to be broken or endured. This was a contract. A transaction. We were being farmed. Two hundred and sixty years of Buena women, each one fed into the same mechanism, each one waiting and wanting and dissolving, and each one producing whatever it was the contract required — time, devotion, surrender, some currency I hadn't named yet.

And behind it: the Hollow Council. Faceless men in dark suits who stood in the fog and recited clauses. Bureaucrats of the supernatural. The system made flesh — or made something that wore the shape of flesh where a face should be.

I set Vivienne's journal on the bed. My room. My floorboards. She'd hidden it here, under the place where I slept, because she knew. She knew the next one would come — the next Aurelia, the next woman in the chain — and she knew where that woman would sleep, because the cottage only had the one bedroom that felt safe. She'd hidden her warning in the one place the system wouldn't think to look, because the system didn't account for women refusing to archive themselves in the attic. The system didn't account for women hiding things under their own beds.
The system didn't account for impatience.

I pressed my palm flat against the floorboard. The cold was still there — mineral, ancient, patient. But I was not. I felt time as a substance under my hands, and for the first time in my life, I didn't want to stretch it. I wanted to compress it. I wanted to take the remaining months of whatever the contract demanded and squeeze them until they screamed, until they collapsed into a singularity of NOW, until there was nothing left to wait for because the waiting was over and the acting had begun.
The curse was patient.
I mustn't be.
